title: The New Rules and CMO Configurations
text:
Core Argument
The new-rules section describes CMO configurations as propositions about what works for whom in what circumstances.

title: The New Rules and CMO Configurations
text:
Study Summary
The new-rules section describes CMO configurations as propositions about what works for whom in what circumstances.
A conjectured configuration is the starting point; a refined configuration is the evaluation finding.
The cumulative ambition is not exact replication of effects but better focusing of mechanisms across settings.

title: Qualitative Research & Evaluation Methods Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Match qualitative methods to questions about meaning, implementation, variation, and unanticipated effects.
Use observation and interviewing as disciplined ways to learn from context, not as informal anecdotes.
Document qualitative evidence systematically enough that interpretation can be challenged and refined.
Connect qualitative findings to decisions, not only to rich description.

title: Qualitative Research & Evaluation Methods Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Program Theory
Program theory is the explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to change. Across the book, it functions as a bridge between design, implementation, monitoring, evaluation, causal inference, and reporting. The recurring discipline is to make assumptions visible enough that they can be discussed, tested, revised, and used.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
section: Theory of Change and Theory of Action
text:
Theory of Change and Theory of Action
The book separates the theory of change from the theory of action. The theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem conditions to intended outcomes. The theory of action explains what the program does to activate that pathway: resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, implementation conditions, and assumptions about factors inside and outside the program’s control.
This distinction matters because a program may have a plausible desired change pathway but weak activities, or strong activities that do not address the causal factors identified in the situation analysis. The two theories need to be checked against each other.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Logic Models and Representation
Logic models are representations of program theory, not the theory itself. Pipeline models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and systems-informed diagrams each foreground different features. The right representation depends on intended use, intervention complexity, and audience.
A good representation explains contribution, makes links meaningful, avoids dead ends, shows direction of change, and removes detail that does not help interpretation.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Assumptions and Context
Assumptions appear throughout the book as testable claims about how change happens, what context permits, and what factors affect success. Program theory is strongest when assumptions are explicit enough to guide evidence collection and humble enough to be revised.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Causal Inference
Program theory supports causal inference by organizing congruence analysis, counterfactual comparison, and critical review. It does not replace causal evidence; it makes causal claims more inspectable by showing what patterns should be observed if the theory is operating.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting

Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 528-545 Chapter 16

This note summarises Chapter 16, “Synthesis and Reporting,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Core Argument
Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Study Summary
The final chapter focuses on how evidence is brought together and reported. For a single evaluation, program theory can organize diverse evidence along the outcomes chain, helping readers see how implementation, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, context, and assumptions relate.
The authors also discuss rapid reporting and software-supported reporting. The key issue is not format but usefulness: reporting should make the evidence traceable to the theory and support decisions while the information is still actionable.
Across evaluations, program theory provides a structure for synthesis. It can connect findings from linked projects, compare evidence across different evaluations, and build cumulative understanding about what works, for whom, why, and under what conditions.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Key Concepts

Theory-based synthesis: integrating evidence according to the causal structure of the program theory.
Rapid reporting: timely communication designed to support ongoing decisions.
Cross-evaluation synthesis: using program theory to compare and integrate findings across studies.
Traceability: making clear how evidence supports conclusions and judgements.

Show where evidence is strong, weak, missing, or contradictory along the causal chain.
Use synthesis to refine theories for future programs and evaluations.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:

This note summarises Chapter 14, “Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Core Argument
Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shows how program theory becomes operational in performance monitoring and evaluation design. It helps decide which aspects of the situation, outcomes, success criteria, assumptions, implementation features, and causal links should be measured.
The authors stress choice. A theory may contain many possible indicators, but a monitoring system must prioritize measures that are feasible, useful, and tied to decisions. Evaluation planning then uses the theory to identify timing, purposes, key questions, information needs, methods, and comparisons.
The chapter also considers complicated and complex programs. In such cases, evaluation may need to examine variation across strands, sites, contexts, or emergent pathways rather than assume one uniform model. Program theory is useful when it guides these design choices rather than becoming a static appendix.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Key Concepts

Performance monitoring: routine tracking of selected aspects of implementation, context, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions.
Evaluation questions: focused questions that determine evidence needs and methods.
Comparison: a design feature used to interpret performance or causal contribution.
Theory-based measurement: choosing measures because they test or illuminate the program theory.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:

Use the theory to decide what not to measure as well as what to measure.
Tie each indicator to a decision, assumption, or causal link.
Design evaluations that can distinguish implementation issues from weak causal theory.

This note summarises Chapter 12, “Some Common Program Archetypes,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Core Argument
Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Study Summary
The chapter organizes recurring program forms such as advisory, information, education, incentives, deterrence, case management, community capacity building, and direct service delivery. These archetypes are useful because similar intervention forms often rely on similar causal assumptions.
Each archetype points to different change mechanisms. Information programs assume attention, comprehension, relevance, and ability to act. Incentives assume behavior responds to rewards. Deterrence assumes detection, sanction, and perceived risk. Case management assumes coordinated support can address multiple barriers. Capacity building assumes communities or organizations can acquire and use new capability.
The authors’ point is not to classify programs for its own sake. Archetypes help evaluators ask sharper questions about whether the chosen strategy fits the problem, what conditions are required, and what evidence would show that the mechanism is operating.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Key Concepts

Program archetype: a recurring intervention pattern with characteristic causal assumptions.
Carrots and sticks: incentive and deterrence approaches to changing behavior.
Case management: coordinated support addressing multiple participant needs or barriers.
Capacity building: strengthening ability to act at individual, organizational, or community levels.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 320-345 Chapter 10

This note summarises Chapter 10, “Critiquing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The chapter provides criteria for reviewing program theory. Internal criteria examine whether the description is clear, whether the outcomes chain organizes the theory, whether outcomes address the problem, whether mechanisms are articulated, and whether the logical argument is plausible.
External validation asks whether the theory is supported by research, consistent with accepted theories, attentive to context, ethically defensible, and appropriate for its intended use. This turns program theory into an evaluand: something that can itself be assessed and improved.
The authors also emphasize stakeholder engagement and response. A critique is valuable only if its results inform revision, evaluation design, or decisions about whether the theory is good enough for the intended purpose.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Internal critique: assessment of coherence, clarity, and logical structure.
External validation: assessment against evidence, accepted theory, context, ethics, and use.
Mechanism articulation: explaining how activities or conditions produce change.
Fitness for purpose: whether the theory is worth its cost for the intended use.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:

The Ethical Base for the Program Theory

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Review program theory before locking in indicators and methods.
Use critique to prioritize evidence gaps and assumptions to test.
Involve stakeholders in judging plausibility, context, and ethical acceptability.

text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 226-267 Chapter 8

This note summarises Chapter 8, “Developing a Theory of Action,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Core Argument
A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Study Summary
Where Chapter 7 concentrates on the desired change pathway, Chapter 8 asks how the program is expected to make that pathway happen. The theory of action identifies resources, activities, outputs, implementation features, assumptions, and external factors that influence whether intended outcomes are achieved.
The chapter gives substantial attention to success criteria and desired attributes. These convert broad outcome language into evaluative standards, making it clearer what would count as good performance. It also emphasizes realistic success criteria, especially when outcomes depend on factors outside program control.
The authors connect theory of action and theory of change through comparison: activities and outputs should be checked against the factors believed to influence outcomes. If the program does not act on the causal factors identified in the theory of change, the model exposes a strategic mismatch.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of action: the account of what the program does and how delivery is expected to support change.
Success criteria: standards or desired attributes used to judge whether outcomes are good enough.
Program factors and nonprogram factors: influences inside and outside the intervention’s control.
Theory matrix: a way to align outcomes, assumptions, activities, and evidence needs.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:

Preparing The Theory Of Action

Assumptions About Nonprogram External Factors

Managing An Identified Nonprogram Factor: Mature Workers Program
How To Identify Factors That Are Likely To Affect Outcomes
Program-Specific Research and Investigation Techniques
Program Theory and General Research
Identifying What The Program Does

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Practical Implications

Define success before interpreting evidence.
Check whether activities are logically connected to the causal factors they are meant to influence.
Track external factors that can enable, constrain, or explain outcomes.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This long chapter sets out process options for identifying or developing program theory. It contrasts staff-led, evaluator-facilitated, stakeholder-engaged, deductive, inductive, and mental-model approaches, arguing that the right mix depends on purpose, context, evidence availability, and stakeholder politics.
The authors give detailed attention to workshops and interviews, but they treat these as tools within a broader design. Workshops can surface divergent theories, build ownership, and negotiate shared models; they can also suppress disagreement if poorly facilitated. Interviews and document review may be better when perspectives are sensitive or dispersed.
The chapter is also realistic about challenges: conflicting program theories, little research evidence, reluctance to disclose assumptions, weak engagement, and limited resources. The response is not to avoid theory development but to make trade-offs explicit and revisit the theory as evidence and circumstances change.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Mental models: implicit stakeholder beliefs about how the program works.
Deductive development: building from formal theory, prior research, or established causal knowledge.
Inductive development: building from observations, cases, data, and practice experience.
Relative program theory: a theory that is fit for a purpose and moment rather than final for all uses.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Process Options For Developing Program Theory
Decision 1: Who Should Be Involved In Developing A Program Theory, And How?

Decisions About Other Stakeholders to Involve

Articulating Stakeholder Mental Models
Deductive Development
Inductive Development
Choosing an Approach: Articulating Mental Models, Deductive Approaches, or Inductive Approaches?
An Iterative Process Combining All Three Approaches To Program Theory Building
Implications of Choice of Approach for Who Should Be Involved
Decision 3: How Might Workshops And Interviews Be Used In Developing Program Theory?
Workshop Processes

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Design the theory-development process as carefully as the representation.
Combine stakeholder knowledge, research evidence, and empirical observation where possible.
Document disagreements and uncertainty instead of forcing premature consensus.

text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

This note summarises Chapter 4, “Scoping Intended Uses,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shifts attention from what a program theory contains to what it is for. A theory intended for early design and stakeholder alignment may need different detail from one used for performance monitoring, impact evaluation, or synthesis across programs.
The authors emphasize users as well as uses. Funders, managers, delivery staff, partners, participants, evaluators, and policymakers may need different representations and may also bring different knowledge about how the intervention is expected to work. Timing matters because a theory built before implementation supports design choices, while a theory reconstructed later supports evaluation and learning from what actually happened.
The chapter presents program theory as a flexible tool across the program cycle. Its usefulness depends on making the intended use explicit before deciding who participates, what evidence is needed, how detailed the model should be, and how formal the representation needs to become.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Key Concepts

Intended users: people who will directly use the program theory or evaluation findings.
Intended uses: the decisions, actions, or learning processes the theory should support.
Timing: the point in the program cycle when the theory is developed or revised.
Use-sensitive design: tailoring theory development and representation to practical use.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Why Intended Use Matters

Using Program Theory for Managing, Engaging Stakeholders, and Communicating
Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation
Using Program Theory for Evidence-Based Policy and Practice
Summary
Exercises

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Practical Implications

Begin each program theory task with a use case, not a template.
Identify who must understand, own, challenge, or apply the theory.
Revise the theory when the program moves from design to implementation to evaluation.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

This note summarises Chapter 2, “Variations of Program Theory over Time,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Core Argument
Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Study Summary
The chapter places program theory in a longer history of evaluation, planning, social science, and management practice. Rather than presenting one settled method, it shows how different traditions have emphasized causal explanation, planning logic, accountability, learning, stakeholder communication, and evidence synthesis.
A substantial part of the chapter is terminological. Terms such as program theory, theory of change, theory of action, logic model, logframe, results chain, outcomes chain, and evaluation model are used differently across organizations and countries. The authors treat terminology as consequential because vocabulary shapes what people attend to and what they overlook.
The chapter distinguishes key ideas without insisting on one universal label. The practical message is to define terms explicitly in each project, align language with users, and avoid assuming that a familiar diagram or phrase carries the same meaning for every stakeholder.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of change: the causal account of how and why change is expected to happen.
Theory of action: the account of what the program will do to activate or support the theory of change.
Logic model: a representation of program components and expected links, often but not always diagrammatic.
Terminological fit: choosing words that make the theory usable for the intended audience.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

A Short History Of Program Theory
Philosophical Roots

The Current State

Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Terminology
Terms for Components in a Pipeline Model
Australia: AusAID (Australian Agency for International Development) (Logframe)
Canada: Treasury
India: Comptroller and Auditor-General
South Africa: Framework for Managing Programme Performance Information, National Treasury
United States: University of Wisconsin Extension Service

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Practical Implications

Define the working vocabulary at the start of a program theory exercise.
Expect organizational templates to encode assumptions about what counts as a good theory.
Treat debates over terms as design signals, not merely semantic disputes.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Core Argument
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Study Summary
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.
The problem is not that logic models are useless, but that they need to be applied with judgment about what the program is trying to change and how stable the causal path is.
When the model cannot represent system dynamics or multiple layers of action, evaluators should supplement it with richer program theory.

text:

Check whether the model represents both the intervention and the system it seeks to change.
Look for confusion caused by compressing multiple causal routes into one linear chain.
Revise the representation before it drives data collection.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Core Argument
The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Study Summary
The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.
A useful design does more than select methods. It asks whether the program is described coherently enough to support evaluation and whether stakeholders need a logic model, a fuller program theory, or both.
The practical lesson is proportionality: choose enough structure to make the evaluation credible and useful without forcing every program into the same template.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:

Practical Implications

Do not move to methods before the program description is adequate.
Use design conversations to surface what stakeholders expect the evaluation to decide.
Escalate from a logic model to program theory when context or causal mechanisms need to be examined.

The guidance treats evaluation as a lifecycle discipline: clarify the intervention and decision need, choose proportionate methods and data, manage quality and ethics, communicate findings transparently, and build capability for use.

title: Core Concepts in Magenta Book
text:
Concepts

Purpose and Use

learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.
evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.
dissemination: Planned communication of findings to audiences who need evidence for decisions, scrutiny, or learning.
transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.



Design and Scoping

scoping: Early work to clarify the intervention, theory of change, users, questions, data, methods, and feasibility.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
evaluation question: A focused question that determines what evidence and analysis an evaluation must provide.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.



Methods and Causality

process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
counterfactual: An estimate of what would have happened without the intervention, used to support impact claims.
theory-based methods: Impact methods that test causal explanations and contribution claims using multiple evidence sources.
experimental methods: Impact designs that use random assignment or controlled rollout to estimate causal effects.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.



Data and Quality

data quality: The fitness of evaluation data, including relevance, validity, completeness, timeliness, and bias control.
data access: The legal, ethical, technical, and practical ability to obtain data needed for evaluation questions.
administrative data: Operational data collected by public services or delivery systems that can support evaluation if fit for purpose.
triangulation: Using multiple evidence sources or methods to test, compare, and strengthen interpretation.
quality assurance: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.



Specialist Guidance

complexity: Conditions such as uncertainty, interdependence, emergence, nonlinearity, and multiple stakeholder perspectives.
realist evaluation: Theory-based evaluation that tests context-mechanism-outcome explanations of what works, for whom, and how.
test and learn: An iterative approach that tests assumptions, learns from real-world feedback, and adapts before scaling.
policy impact evaluation: Impact evaluation applied to government policy, with attention to attribution, design quality, and feasibility.
qualitative quality: Judgment about whether qualitative design, sampling, data, analysis, and reporting are fit for purpose.
open science: Practices that make research plans, materials, analysis, and outputs more transparent and accessible.

title: Chapter 7: Evaluation Capabilities
text:
Core Argument
Government evaluation quality depends on capability across scoping, leadership, methods, use, dissemination, and the ability to work with uncertainty.

title: Chapter 7: Evaluation Capabilities
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 7 summarises the capabilities needed to design, manage, and use evaluations. It groups capability around scoping, leading and managing, methods, and use and dissemination.
Scoping capability includes understanding rationale, constructing a theory of change, identifying a suitable approach, and producing a proportionate plan. Leading capability includes maintaining momentum, influencing stakeholders, adapting to change, and acting with integrity.
Methods capability spans monitoring data, administrative data, primary research, process evaluation, theory-based impact evaluation, experimental and quasi-experimental approaches, value for money work, synthesis, and appropriate awareness of AI-assisted analysis.
Use and dissemination capability means turning evidence into clear products, policy implications, publication routes, and stakeholder communication that make findings usable without overstating certainty.
For close reading, track how this note uses capability, scoping, theory of change, and mixed methods while moving through scoping capability, leading and managing, and methods. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 7: Evaluation Capabilities
text:
Key Concepts

capability: The knowledge, skills, roles, and behaviours required to scope, manage, deliver, and use evaluation well.
scoping: Early work to clarify the intervention, theory of change, users, questions, data, methods, and feasibility.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
mixed methods: Use of qualitative, quantitative, and other evidence sources together to answer complementary questions.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
synthesis: Combining evidence from multiple sources or studies to answer evaluation questions and support judgment.
evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.

title: Chapter 7: Evaluation Capabilities
text:
Chapter Structure

Scoping capability.
Leading and managing.
Methods.
Use and dissemination.
Further detail.

title: Chapter 7: Evaluation Capabilities
text:
Practical Implications

Assess team capability against the evaluation’s actual methods and use demands.
Bring in specialist expertise when the design needs methods, data, or ethical skills the core team lacks.
Treat capability building as part of institutional evaluation quality, not only individual training.

title: Chapter 5: Managing an Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Managing evaluation is an active governance task that protects usefulness, feasibility, quality, ethics, and adaptation across the evaluation lifecycle.

title: Chapter 5: Managing an Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 5 moves from design to delivery management. A well-run evaluation needs clear governance, agreed roles, realistic timetables, quality assurance, stakeholder access, and alignment with intervention decisions.
Steering groups are more than ceremonial. They review feasibility, provide access, quality assure design and tools, advise on changing circumstances, review emerging findings, and help ensure the work remains policy relevant.
The chapter recognises tension between consistency and flexibility. Some designs require stable delivery and measurement, while complex or innovative interventions may need planned review points so evaluation and implementation can adapt without losing credibility.
Quality assurance and ethics are continuous responsibilities. The project manager must plan independent scrutiny, analytical checks, peer review, objective reporting, participant protection, data governance, and appropriate use of emerging tools.
For close reading, track how this note uses steering group, evaluation plan, quality assurance, and adaptive management while moving through governance, linking evaluation to intervention design, and supplier and stakeholder management. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 5: Managing an Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

steering group: A governance body that reviews design, quality, feasibility, emerging findings, and use.
evaluation plan: A practical specification of questions, methods, data, responsibilities, timing, resources, and use.
quality assurance: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
adaptive management: Management that expects learning, review, and adjustment as intervention context and evidence change.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.
governance: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
benefits management: Project discipline for defining, measuring, and realising intended benefits while evaluation tests contribution and value.

title: Chapter 5: Managing an Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Governance.
Linking evaluation to intervention design.
Supplier and stakeholder management.
Flexibility and consistency.
Quality assurance.
Ethics.

title: Chapter 5: Managing an Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Create governance that can genuinely steer design, access, quality, and use.
Build planned adaptation points into evaluations of changing or innovative interventions.
Treat quality assurance and ethics as lifecycle responsibilities, not final report checks.

title: Chapter 3: Evaluation Methods
text:
Core Argument
Method choice should follow the evaluation questions, theory of change, assumptions, feasibility, and stakeholder need for causal, process, synthesis, or value for money evidence.

title: Chapter 3: Evaluation Methods
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 3 surveys methods for government evaluation and groups them around generic research methods, theory-based impact evaluation, experimental and quasi-experimental impact evaluation, value for money analysis, and synthesis.
The chapter warns against method-led evaluation. No single method answers every evaluation question, and most evaluations need mixed methods because process, impact, and economic questions illuminate different parts of the intervention.
Theory-based methods are used when causal explanation, mechanisms, context, and contribution matter. Experimental and quasi-experimental methods are used when credible comparison is feasible and the evaluation needs stronger attribution.
Value for money methods need to be planned early and aligned with appraisal and impact evidence. Synthesis methods help combine existing evidence, but the suitability of each approach depends on the evidence base and evaluation purpose.
For close reading, track how this note uses mixed methods, theory-based methods, experimental methods, and quasi-experimental methods while moving through method selection, generic research methods, and theory-based impact methods. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 3: Evaluation Methods
text:
Key Concepts

mixed methods: Use of qualitative, quantitative, and other evidence sources together to answer complementary questions.
theory-based methods: Impact methods that test causal explanations and contribution claims using multiple evidence sources.
experimental methods: Impact designs that use random assignment or controlled rollout to estimate causal effects.
quasi-experimental methods: Impact designs that construct credible comparison groups without random assignment.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
synthesis: Combining evidence from multiple sources or studies to answer evaluation questions and support judgment.
causal attribution: Reasoning about whether observed outcomes were caused by the intervention rather than other factors.

title: Chapter 3: Evaluation Methods
text:
Chapter Structure

Method selection.
Generic research methods.
Theory-based impact methods.
Experimental and quasi-experimental methods.
Value for money methods.
Synthesis methods.

title: Chapter 3: Evaluation Methods
text:
Practical Implications

Choose methods after clarifying questions, theory, assumptions, and feasible data.
Use mixed methods when implementation, impact, and value questions all matter.
Make tradeoffs explicit when cost, timing, ethics, or respondent burden limit the strongest method.

title: Chapter 1: Why, How and When to Evaluate?
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation should be planned early, proportionately, and around real decisions so government can learn, account for public spending, and improve outcomes.

title: Chapter 1: Why, How and When to Evaluate?
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 1 explains evaluation as planned inquiry into whether an intervention is working, why it is working or not, for whom, and at what value. It is written for decision-makers and analysts, but also signals expectations to suppliers and external commissioners.
The chapter ties evaluation to the policy and project lifecycle. Evaluation needs should be considered during business case development, intervention design, monitoring, benefits management, and delivery, because waiting until the end can leave too little data or too few comparison options.
A good evaluation is useful, credible, robust, proportionate, and transparent. Those qualities shape decisions about independence, stakeholder needs, data quality, method choice, communication, and the limits that must accompany findings.
The chapter also distinguishes users of evaluation evidence. Ministers, accounting officers, policy teams, delivery teams, finance professionals, analysts, scrutiny bodies, the public, and researchers may need different products and timing from the same evaluation.
For close reading, track how this note uses learning, accountability, benefits management, and evaluation use while moving through what policy evaluation is, why evaluate, and when to plan evaluation. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 1: Why, How and When to Evaluate?
text:
Key Concepts

learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.
benefits management: Project discipline for defining, measuring, and realising intended benefits while evaluation tests contribution and value.
evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.
transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.

title: Chapter 1: Why, How and When to Evaluate?
text:
Chapter Structure

What policy evaluation is.
Why evaluate.
When to plan evaluation.
Benefits management and monitoring.
Good evaluation principles.
Users and stages.

title: Chapter 1: Why, How and When to Evaluate?
text:
Practical Implications

Build evaluation expectations into business cases and delivery plans before data options close.
Clarify who will use findings and what decision or scrutiny need they serve.
Use quality principles to choose a feasible design rather than maximising technical ambition in the abstract.

title: Test and Learn
text:
Core Argument
The Test and Learn annex shows how structured, iterative testing can build evidence, reduce uncertainty, and improve policy before full-scale evaluation or rollout.

title: Test and Learn
text:
Study Summary
Test and Learn is presented as a way of working rather than a separate evaluation type. It embeds structured testing, feedback, and adaptation into policy and service design.
The approach is most useful when problems are complex or uncertain, assumptions are untested, and teams need rapid learning before committing to scale. It is less useful when requirements and solutions are already fixed.
Test and Learn can strengthen later evaluation by clarifying theory of change, testing critical assumptions, improving delivery, and identifying where counterfactual or theory-based impact methods may be feasible.
The annex links testing to robust evaluation by encouraging teams to define uncertainties, collect real-world evidence, examine context, and decide when to grow, adapt, stop, or move to formal evaluation.
For close reading, track how this note uses test and learn, adaptive management, theory of change, and learning while moving through introduction, when to use test and learn, and testing assumptions. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Test and Learn
text:
Key Concepts

test and learn: An iterative approach that tests assumptions, learns from real-world feedback, and adapts before scaling.
adaptive management: Management that expects learning, review, and adjustment as intervention context and evidence change.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
counterfactual: An estimate of what would have happened without the intervention, used to support impact claims.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.

title: Test and Learn
text:
Appendix Structure

Introduction.
When to use test and learn.
Testing assumptions.
Supporting robust evaluation.
Grow and adapt decisions.
Resources.

title: Test and Learn
text:
Practical Implications

Use Test and Learn before scaling when assumptions are uncertain and adaptation is still possible.
Treat small tests as evidence-building exercises, not informal pilots without decision rules.
Carry learning from tests into later process, impact, and value for money evaluation designs.

title: Realist Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The realist evaluation guide explains how to test and refine context-mechanism-outcome theories when evaluators need to know what works, for whom, how, and under what conditions.

title: Realist Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
The realist guide positions realist evaluation as a theory-based approach for programmes where average effects are not enough. It asks how the intervention creates opportunities that people respond to differently in different contexts.
The central explanatory unit is the context-mechanism-outcome configuration. The intervention does not act mechanically; outcomes arise when people respond to resources, opportunities, incentives, or constraints in a context.
Realist evaluation develops, tests, and refines hypotheses. It begins with programme theory, translates that theory into realist terms, collects evidence about how mechanisms operate, and revises explanations as evidence accumulates.
The guide is especially useful where programmes are new, being scaled, producing mixed outcomes, or operating in complex systems where understanding why and for whom matters for future adaptation.
For close reading, track how this note uses realist evaluation, theory-based methods, context, and mechanism while moving through what realist evaluation is, introducing the realist frame, and context-mechanism-outcome theory. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Realist Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

realist evaluation: Theory-based evaluation that tests context-mechanism-outcome explanations of what works, for whom, and how.
theory-based methods: Impact methods that test causal explanations and contribution claims using multiple evidence sources.
context: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
mechanism: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
outcome: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
causal attribution: Reasoning about whether observed outcomes were caused by the intervention rather than other factors.
complexity: Conditions such as uncertainty, interdependence, emergence, nonlinearity, and multiple stakeholder perspectives.

title: Realist Evaluation
text:
Appendix Structure

What realist evaluation is.
Introducing the realist frame.
Context-mechanism-outcome theory.
Realist cycle.
Examples and resources.

title: Realist Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Use realist evaluation when adaptation and transfer depend on understanding mechanisms in context.
Write causal hypotheses in a form that can be tested with evidence.
Avoid reducing realist work to broad narrative explanation without explicit CMO testing.

title: Quality in Policy Impact Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The policy impact quality guide helps users judge how strongly an impact design can support attribution and what conclusions are warranted.

title: Quality in Policy Impact Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
This appendix focuses on empirical impact evaluations that quantify whether observed outcomes were caused by a policy. It aims to help policy makers and analysts choose designs with clear strengths and limits.
The central issue is attribution. Monitoring outcomes can show change, but it cannot by itself establish whether the policy caused that change; stronger designs need a credible counterfactual.
Design quality has to be proportionate to the policy’s risk, scale, profile, and learning value. The guide asks users to consider whether they can afford not to conduct a robust impact evaluation when causal claims matter.
The guide also recognises feasibility limits. Legal constraints, national rollout, small sample sizes, weak data, and statutory frameworks may restrict design options, but those constraints do not change what can validly be concluded.
For close reading, track how this note uses policy impact evaluation, causal attribution, counterfactual, and internal validity while moving through purpose, impact questions, and quality and attribution. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Quality in Policy Impact Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

policy impact evaluation: Impact evaluation applied to government policy, with attention to attribution, design quality, and feasibility.
causal attribution: Reasoning about whether observed outcomes were caused by the intervention rather than other factors.
counterfactual: An estimate of what would have happened without the intervention, used to support impact claims.
internal validity: The credibility of the causal estimate for the intervention and setting being evaluated.
external validity: The extent to which impact findings can inform decisions beyond the specific evaluated setting.
robustness: The extent to which findings remain credible under reasonable checks, assumptions, and alternative analyses.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Quality in Policy Impact Evaluation
text:
Appendix Structure

Purpose.
Impact questions.
Quality and attribution.
Design hierarchy.
Feasibility constraints.
Interpretation limits.

title: Quality in Policy Impact Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Match causal claims to the design’s ability to construct a credible counterfactual.
Be explicit when feasibility constraints limit attribution strength.
Plan impact evaluation early enough to avoid avoidable data and rollout constraints.

title: Guidance on the Impact Evaluation of AI Interventions
text:
Core Argument
The AI impact evaluation guide applies Magenta Book impact principles to AI interventions whose novelty, digital rollout, risks, and iteration create both opportunities and evaluation challenges.

title: Guidance on the Impact Evaluation of AI Interventions
text:
Study Summary
This guide argues that the core principles of impact evaluation still apply to AI interventions, but the intervention context adds distinctive risks, opportunities, and feasibility issues.
AI interventions often have high uncertainty, high learning value, and potential public trust implications. That means they may warrant more substantial evaluation than routine digital changes of similar cost.
The digital nature of many AI interventions can make randomisation, phased rollout, and data collection more feasible. At the same time, iteration, changing models, public attitudes, bias, and unintended harms require broader theory and process evidence.
The guide encourages evaluators to plan early, define objectives and theory of change, choose experimental, quasi-experimental, or theory-based designs as appropriate, and use case examples to reason through data and ethics.
For close reading, track how this note uses impact evaluation, AI intervention, experimental methods, and quasi-experimental methods while moving through summary principles, introduction, and opportunities and challenges. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Guidance on the Impact Evaluation of AI Interventions
text:
Key Concepts

impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
AI intervention: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
experimental methods: Impact designs that use random assignment or controlled rollout to estimate causal effects.
quasi-experimental methods: Impact designs that construct credible comparison groups without random assignment.
theory-based methods: Impact methods that test causal explanations and contribution claims using multiple evidence sources.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.
public trust: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.

title: Guidance on the Impact Evaluation of AI Interventions
text:
Appendix Structure

Summary principles.
Introduction.
Opportunities and challenges.
Choosing approaches.
Public attitudes and risks.
Hypothetical case studies.
Glossary.

title: Guidance on the Impact Evaluation of AI Interventions
text:
Practical Implications

Plan AI evaluation alongside design and rollout so randomisation or phased access remains possible.
Treat bias, public attitudes, explainability, and unintended effects as evaluation issues, not only technical assurance issues.
Combine quantitative impact evidence with theory-based and qualitative evidence when AI mechanisms or user responses matter.

title: Annex A: Analytical Methods for Use Within an Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Annex A extends Chapter 3 by giving a method catalogue for theory-based impact evaluation, counterfactual designs, value for money analysis, synthesis, and generic research methods.

title: Annex A: Analytical Methods for Use Within an Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Annex A is the technical method companion to the main methods chapter. It groups approaches by the kind of evaluation question and causal claim they can support.
Theory-based methods help evaluators test causal explanations when mechanisms, context, contribution, and complexity matter. Experimental and quasi-experimental methods help estimate effects when credible comparison is feasible.
Value for money methods are presented as more than a benefit-cost ratio. They can combine economic appraisal, distributional evidence, qualitative judgment, and criteria where monetisation is incomplete.
Synthesis and generic research methods support both process and impact evaluation by combining existing studies, monitoring, observation, surveys, interviews, and other evidence sources.
For close reading, track how this note uses theory-based methods, experimental methods, quasi- experimental methods, and value for money while moving through A1 theory-based methods, A2 experimental and quasi-experimental methods, and A3 value for money methods. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Annex A: Analytical Methods for Use Within an Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

theory-based methods: Impact methods that test causal explanations and contribution claims using multiple evidence sources.
experimental methods: Impact designs that use random assignment or controlled rollout to estimate causal effects.
quasi-experimental methods: Impact designs that construct credible comparison groups without random assignment.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
synthesis: Combining evidence from multiple sources or studies to answer evaluation questions and support judgment.
mixed methods: Use of qualitative, quantitative, and other evidence sources together to answer complementary questions.
realist evaluation: Theory-based evaluation that tests context-mechanism-outcome explanations of what works, for whom, and how.

title: Annex A: Analytical Methods for Use Within an Evaluation
text:
Appendix Structure

A1 theory-based methods.
A2 experimental and quasi-experimental methods.
A3 value for money methods.
A4 synthesis methods.
A5 generic research methods.

title: Annex A: Analytical Methods for Use Within an Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Use Annex A as a method-selection reference after evaluation questions are prioritised.
Check each method’s assumptions, data needs, and limits before treating it as feasible.
Combine approaches when the evaluation needs both attribution and explanation.

title: Constructing a Theory of Change
text:
Core Argument
The section treats theory of change as an early design step for causal evaluation.

title: Constructing a Theory of Change
text:
Study Summary
The section treats theory of change as an early design step for causal evaluation.
A theory of change maps the causal logic linking program inputs, activities, outputs, behavior change, and outcomes.
Working through this logic with stakeholders can clarify both the evaluation question and the program design itself.

Surface assumptions that could invalidate the impact claim.
Revise the evaluation question if the causal pathway is unclear.

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the chapter notes from Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications (Stufflebeam and Coryn 2014).

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Start every evaluation by clarifying the evaluand, users, purposes, value criteria, standards, and intended uses.
Choose models by fit to the assignment and standards, not by brand familiarity or method preference.
Distinguish focused studies from full evaluations of merit and worth, especially when using quasi-evaluation approaches.
Build standards into design, budgeting, contracting, information collection, reporting, and metaevaluation.
Use multiple sources and methods when a single procedure cannot support the required judgment.
Treat analysis and synthesis as value work: evidence must be connected to criteria before conclusions are justified.
Plan communication and follow-up early so findings reach intended users in forms they can use.
Use metaevaluation proportionate to stakes, risk, and public reliance on the evaluation.
For organizations, move from ad hoc studies toward mainstreamed evaluation systems with periodic review.

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as checks during scoping, proposal decisions, design review, method selection, reporting, follow-up, and metaevaluation. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s context, available resources, stakeholder needs, and standards obligations.

title: Core Concepts in Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications
text:
Central Logic
The book treats evaluation as a standards-guided practice for producing defensible judgments of value and helping users improve programs, make decisions, and meet accountability obligations.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Organizations should move evaluation from occasional projects to a mainstreamed system for planning, accountability, learning, and improvement.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 26 closes the book by shifting from individual evaluation studies to organizational evaluation systems. The authors argue that organizations should institutionalize and mainstream evaluation so that it becomes part of routine governance and improvement.
Institutionalizing evaluation means establishing a durable system, roles, standards, policies, resources, and review mechanisms. Mainstreaming evaluation means embedding use of that system across levels and functions rather than leaving evaluation in a specialist corner.
The chapter reviews the book’s themes and translates them into organizational principles. Evaluation systems should be grounded in theory, standards, stakeholder engagement, sound methods, metaevaluation, communication, and systematic use.
A central practical tool is a checklist for establishing or strengthening an organizational evaluation system. The notes do not reproduce the checklist, but they treat it as a staged planning aid for design teams, review teams, standards selection, communication, capacity building, and periodic review.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses institutionalizing evaluation, mainstreaming evaluation, evaluation system, and design team while moving through review of book themes, definitions and rationale, and early efforts and advances. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Five, this chapter asks how evaluation itself is judged, governed, and made durable. It connects the book’s standards language to organizational routines so that evaluation quality is not left to isolated projects or individual evaluator preference.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

institutionalizing evaluation: Creating durable policies, roles, standards, resources, and routines that make evaluation part of governance.
mainstreaming evaluation: Embedding evaluation expectations and use across normal organizational planning, management, and accountability.
evaluation system: An organization’s durable arrangements for commissioning, conducting, reviewing, using, and improving evaluation.
design team: A group responsible for shaping or strengthening an evaluation system, plan, or institutional approach.
review team: A group responsible for reviewing evaluation plans, reports, systems, standards, or quality.
organizational standards: Criteria, policies, and expectations an organization adopts to guide and judge its evaluation system.
capacity building: Developing the people, routines, policies, and skills that let an organization use evaluation repeatedly.
periodic review: Scheduled reassessment of evaluation systems, procedures, or studies to maintain quality and usefulness.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Review of book themes.
Definitions and rationale.
Early efforts and advances.

Conclusions.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Treat evaluation capacity as an organizational system, not a series of ad hoc studies.
Assign responsibility for standards, review, communication, and use.
Periodically evaluate the evaluation system itself and revise it.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation findings have value only when communicated to intended users in credible, timely, accessible, and follow-up-supported ways.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 24 argues that sound design, data collection, and analysis can still fail if findings are not communicated effectively. The evaluator must understand intended users, their information rights, their intended uses, and the formats that will support action.
Communication begins before the final report. Interim feedback can help improve programs, test interpretations, surface errors, and sustain use. The chapter links this especially to approaches such as CIPP, responsive evaluation, and utilization-focused evaluation.
Final reporting should synthesize findings around evaluation questions and value judgments rather than merely list methods or data sources. Reports may need different layers, formats, technical appendices, oral presentations, workshops, or other media.
Follow-up support is part of evaluation impact. Evaluators may need to help users interpret findings, plan responses, communicate with stakeholders, and document actions taken while preserving the boundaries of their role.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses intended users, interim feedback, final report, and dissemination while moving through reporting challenges, conditions for use, and interim feedback. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Key Concepts

intended users: The people expected to engage with and act on the evaluation’s process or findings.
interim feedback: Evaluation communication delivered during implementation so users can correct, learn, or adapt before final reporting.
final report: A culminating communication of evaluation evidence, interpretation, conclusions, limitations, and recommendations.
dissemination: The planned distribution of findings to audiences who need access to evaluation evidence and conclusions.
reporting rights: Agreed authority over review, release, dissemination, confidentiality, and publication of evaluation findings.
follow-up support: Post-report assistance that helps users interpret findings, plan responses, and sustain appropriate use.
evaluation use: The ways intended users apply evaluation processes or findings to decisions, learning, accountability, or improvement.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Chapter Structure

Reporting challenges.
Conditions for use.
Interim feedback.
Final report preparation.
Delivery and follow-up.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Practical Implications

Plan report audiences and formats during design and contracting.
Use interim feedback to support correction without compromising final judgment.
Organize reports around questions and conclusions, not data-collection silos.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Core Argument
Evaluative conclusions are only as defensible as the scope, quality, reliability, validity, and management of the information collected.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 22 gives practical advice on information collection. It begins from standards because the credibility of answers depends on whether information is relevant, sufficient, reliable, valid, and responsibly managed.
The chapter proposes a broad information framework that can cover background, context, structure, operations, costs, outputs, outcomes, side effects, and stakeholder perspectives. The goal is to avoid collecting only what is easy or familiar.
Reliability and validity are treated as practical obligations. Evaluators should train collectors, document procedures, use multiple sources and methods when needed, check data quality, protect records, and report threats to validity.
The chapter also recognizes feasibility. Adequate scope must be balanced against burden, time, and cost. The evaluator’s job is to gather enough credible information to answer priority questions and support justified conclusions.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluative information, information scope, reliability, and validity while moving through standards for collection, information collection framework, and sampling. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Key Concepts

evaluative information: Information collected because it can support a judgment about merit, worth, significance, probity, or use.
information scope: The range of context, inputs, activities, costs, outputs, outcomes, side effects, and perspectives considered.
reliability: The consistency and dependability of measurement, coding, data collection, or judgment procedures.
validity: The degree to which evidence and interpretations support the claims being made.
sampling: The selection of cases, people, sites, records, or observations from which evaluation evidence will be collected.
information management: Procedures for organizing, protecting, documenting, storing, retrieving, and controlling evaluation information.
multiple methods: Use of more than one evidence source or method to strengthen coverage, triangulation, or credibility.
data quality: The fitness of data for evaluation use, including relevance, completeness, reliability, validity, and traceability.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Chapter Structure

Standards for collection.
Information collection framework.
Sampling.
Reliability and validity.
Collection techniques.
Information management.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Practical Implications

Plan information collection against each evaluation question and value criterion.
Use multiple sources when single procedures cannot carry the claim.
Document threats to validity and the steps taken to reduce them.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation budgets are ethical and design instruments: they translate the promised scope of work into feasible, transparent, and defensible resource commitments.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 20 explains why budgeting is inseparable from evaluation design. A budget should estimate the resources needed to deliver the agreed design and should make assumptions about tasks, personnel, travel, support, timing, indirect costs, and sponsor contributions visible.
The chapter distinguishes fixed and responsive conditions. Preordinate evaluations may support detailed up-front budgets, while responsive evaluations often need general budgets and periodic updating as user needs and interim findings develop.
Ethical budgeting matters. Underbudgeting can make a promised design impossible; padding or hiding costs can undermine trust; vague agreements can produce conflict. The authors treat budgeting as part of professional integrity.
The generic budgeting checklist is meant for constructing and reviewing budgets. It asks evaluators to link cost categories to design tasks, identify the needed level of detail, determine cost factors, and revisit the budget as plans become clearer.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation budget, line-item budget, modular budget, and cost factor while moving through budget rationale, ethical principles, and illustrative budgets. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Key Concepts

evaluation budget: A resource plan that links evaluation tasks, staffing, timing, travel, support, and deliverables to cost.
line-item budget: A budget format that itemizes costs by categories such as personnel, travel, materials, and indirect costs.
modular budget: A budget format that groups costs into larger modules or rounded categories rather than detailed line items.
cost factor: A task, assumption, rate, or condition that drives the cost of evaluation work.
grant: A funding arrangement that supports evaluation or program work under specified sponsor conditions.
contract: A binding agreement that specifies evaluation scope, deliverables, resources, rights, and responsibilities.
cooperative agreement: A funding or working arrangement that involves substantial sponsor and evaluator cooperation.
ethical budgeting: Budgeting that honestly matches promised work to feasible resources and makes uncertainty explicit.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:

Budget rationale.
Ethical principles.
Illustrative budgets.
Budget dimensions.
Generic budgeting checklist.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Practical Implications

Build the budget from tasks in the evaluation design.
Make uncertainty explicit in responsive or evolving evaluations.
Use the budget as a feasibility test before finalizing promises.
