title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Metaevaluation is the professional requirement to evaluate evaluations for improvement, accountability, and public trust.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 25 defines metaevaluation as evaluating evaluations. Metaevaluators can support evaluators formatively by reviewing plans, procedures, reports, and impacts, and summatively by judging the completed evaluation’s utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, and accountability.
The standards-and-metaevaluation seed note belongs here because this chapter turns standards into a direct quality-assurance process. Standards-based metaevaluation is treated as analogous to auditing in accounting: a profession should not ask society to trust its products without independent scrutiny.
The chapter presents tasks, arrangements, cases, checklists, and qualifications for metaevaluation. It distinguishes metaevaluation from meta-analysis: the former judges an evaluation’s quality, while the latter synthesizes results across studies.
The authors also add judgment about scale and context. Not every small local improvement study needs an independent metaevaluation, but high-stakes, public, controversial, or widely used evaluations require stronger review.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses metaevaluation, formative metaevaluation, summative metaevaluation, and standards-based review while moving through rationale and definition, standards and caveats, and responsibilities. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Five, this chapter asks how evaluation itself is judged, governed, and made durable. It connects the book’s standards language to organizational routines so that evaluation quality is not left to isolated projects or individual evaluator preference.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Key Concepts

metaevaluation: The evaluation of an evaluation’s design, conduct, reporting, utility, propriety, accuracy, and accountability.
formative metaevaluation: Quality review conducted during an evaluation so defects can be corrected before completion.
summative metaevaluation: Quality review conducted after completion to judge the evaluation’s overall merit and accountability.
standards-based review: Review of an evaluation plan, process, or report using explicit professional standards.
evaluation accountability: The requirement that evaluation work itself be documented, reviewable, and judged against quality standards.
meta-analysis: Statistical or systematic synthesis of findings across studies; distinct from metaevaluation of evaluation quality.
comparative metaevaluation: Review that compares multiple evaluations or evaluation systems against common quality criteria.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Chapter Structure

Rationale and definition.
Standards and caveats.
Responsibilities.
Tasks and checklists.
Cases and comparative metaevaluation.
Context and constraints.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Practical Implications

Decide metaevaluation intensity based on stakes, audience, and risk.
Use standards to review plans before flaws become final findings.
Report limitations and quality judgments transparently.

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Chapter Map
text:
How to Use This Map

Start with Part One to ground definitions, standards, and theory.
Use Part Two to compare evaluation approaches and understand the authors’ standards-based judgments.
Use Part Three when choosing among selected models such as CIPP, consumer-oriented, responsive, or utilization-focused evaluation.
Use Part Four as a procedural guide for scoping, designing, budgeting, contracting, collecting, analyzing, reporting, and follow-up.
Use Part Five to check evaluation quality and organizational evaluation capacity.

Study Summary
The section assesses evaluation theory as a field rich in concepts, standards, and approaches but still developing stronger validated theory.
It argues for converting useful approaches into better-tested theories through research and metaevaluation.
The practical implication is humility: models should be used critically and improved through evidence.

title: Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Balance rigor, cost, timing, and likely usefulness.
Fit the evaluation design to the program’s life cycle and decision context.
Treat performance measurement and evaluation as complementary evidence systems.
Use stakeholder engagement to sharpen, not dilute, evaluation focus.

Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: What Is Impact Evaluation?
text:
Core Argument
The section distinguishes monitoring, evaluation, and impact evaluation as related but different evidence functions.

title: What Is Impact Evaluation?
text:
Study Summary
The section distinguishes monitoring, evaluation, and impact evaluation as related but different evidence functions.
Monitoring tracks program activity and performance over time; evaluation answers selected questions about design, implementation, and results.
Impact evaluation focuses on the causal effect of a program or design innovation on outcomes of interest.

Use monitoring data as context and operational evidence for the impact study.
State clearly which outcomes the causal claim will cover.

The section explains why before-and-after observation alone is not sufficient for causal inference.
Impact evaluation requires an estimate of what would have happened to participants without the program.
The central design problem is therefore to construct a credible counterfactual.

title: Government Analytical Evaluation Capabilities Framework
text:
Core Argument
The capabilities framework translates evaluation quality into the skills, behaviours, and development actions government analysts need across the evaluation lifecycle.

title: Government Analytical Evaluation Capabilities Framework
text:
Study Summary
The framework is a workforce and project-planning tool. It describes the capabilities needed to scope, lead, manage, deliver, and use evaluation in government settings.
It can be used for self-assessment, team planning, departmental capability review, and identifying training needs. The point is to make evaluation capability visible before a project fails because the right skills were missing.
The capability areas mirror the main guidance: scoping and theory of change, leadership and management, methods and data, process and impact evaluation, value for money, synthesis, and use of findings.
The framework also points analysts toward resources for capability development, making it a practical bridge between guidance and professional development.
For close reading, track how this note uses capability, scoping, theory of change, and administrative data while moving through framework purpose, self-assessment, and scoping capabilities. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Government Analytical Evaluation Capabilities Framework
text:
Key Concepts

capability: The knowledge, skills, roles, and behaviours required to scope, manage, deliver, and use evaluation well.
scoping: Early work to clarify the intervention, theory of change, users, questions, data, methods, and feasibility.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
administrative data: Operational data collected by public services or delivery systems that can support evaluation if fit for purpose.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.

title: Government Analytical Evaluation Capabilities Framework
text:
Appendix Structure

Framework purpose.
Self-assessment.
Scoping capabilities.
Leading and managing.
Methods capabilities.
Use and dissemination.

title: Government Analytical Evaluation Capabilities Framework
text:
Practical Implications

Use capability review before commissioning to identify gaps in methods, data, leadership, or use.
Build teams around the evaluation design rather than expecting one analyst to cover every domain.
Link professional development to live evaluation risks and departmental capability needs.

title: Handling Complexity in Policy Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The complexity guide shows how evaluation design, commissioning, management, and methods must adapt when interventions operate in uncertain, changing, and interdependent systems.

title: Handling Complexity in Policy Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
This guide explains why complex policy settings weaken simple assumptions about stable interventions, linear causation, and fixed evaluation plans. Multiple perspectives, system responses, and changing context affect both delivery and evidence.
Commissioning and management need more learning, reflection, and flexibility than routine evaluations. Commissioners may need iterative designs, regular review points, more stakeholder mapping, and procurement arrangements that allow adaptation.
Theory of change work remains important but may need systems mapping, participatory approaches, or nonlinear causal models. Evaluations may need to examine how the intervention and surrounding system evolve together.
The guide links method choice to purpose and system attributes. Complexity-appropriate evaluation can still support accountability, but it often needs a stronger learning function and more explicit treatment of uncertainty.
For close reading, track how this note uses complexity, adaptive management, theory of change, and systems mapping while moving through why complexity matters, evaluation challenges, and commissioning and management. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Handling Complexity in Policy Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

complexity: Conditions such as uncertainty, interdependence, emergence, nonlinearity, and multiple stakeholder perspectives.
adaptive management: Management that expects learning, review, and adjustment as intervention context and evidence change.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
systems mapping: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
stakeholder engagement: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Handling Complexity in Policy Evaluation
text:
Appendix Structure

Why complexity matters.
Evaluation challenges.
Commissioning and management.
Building understanding.
Complexity-appropriate approaches.
Resources.

title: Handling Complexity in Policy Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Diagnose complexity before specifying fixed methods, budgets, and deliverables.
Build review points into governance so emerging evidence can reshape the evaluation design.
Use systems and stakeholder mapping where linear programme models would hide important causal dynamics.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The qualitative quality guide provides a framework for judging whether qualitative evaluation evidence is defensible, transparent, useful, and fit for purpose.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
This appendix gives a framework for appraising qualitative evaluations, especially those concerned with policy, programmes, and practice. It is built from literature review, government evaluation practice, existing quality frameworks, and expert consultation.
The framework is mainly designed for assessing written outputs, but selected questions can also inform proposals, protocols, training, and research management.
Quality assessment focuses on the findings and on the research process behind them: design, sampling, data collection, analysis, reporting, reflexivity, ethics, neutrality, and auditability.
The guide emphasises professional judgment. Quality indicators support appraisal, but the assessor still has to decide what matters most for the evaluation’s purpose, method, and policy context.
For close reading, track how this note uses qualitative quality, data quality, professional judgment, and auditability while moving through framework purpose, scope, and application. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

qualitative quality: Judgment about whether qualitative design, sampling, data, analysis, and reporting are fit for purpose.
data quality: The fitness of evaluation data, including relevance, validity, completeness, timeliness, and bias control.
professional judgment: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
auditability: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
reflexivity: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.
fitness for purpose: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:

Framework purpose.
Scope.
Application.
Guiding principles.
Appraisal questions.
Quality indicators.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Assess qualitative findings together with the design and analysis process that produced them.
Use quality questions to strengthen proposals and protocols, not only completed reports.
Keep judgment proportionate to the evaluation purpose and method rather than applying a mechanical checklist.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Core Argument
The PIR guide adapts Magenta Book evaluation logic to regulatory review, where proportional evidence, monitoring data, stakeholder evidence, and feasible counterfactuals are central.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Study Summary
The regulatory post implementation review guide focuses on evaluating whether regulation remains justified, effective, proportionate, and fit for purpose after implementation.
Preparation involves clarifying the measure, baseline, intended effects, monitoring arrangements, existing evidence, proportionality, and review obligations. Where early planning was missed, the guide still pushes reviewers to build the best feasible evidence base.
PIR methods often combine monitoring data, stakeholder engagement, process evidence, impact evidence, and value for money considerations. Strong counterfactual impact designs may be difficult when regulation applies to all affected parties, so limitations need to be explicit.
Case material is used to show how different review strategies can meet scrutiny needs when evidence, timing, or implementation constraints vary.
For close reading, track how this note uses monitoring, counterfactual, impact evaluation, and process evaluation while moving through glossary and scope, preparing for a PIR, and monitoring data. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Key Concepts

monitoring: Routine tracking of delivery, outputs, performance, or benefits that can inform but does not replace evaluation.
counterfactual: An estimate of what would have happened without the intervention, used to support impact claims.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Appendix Structure

Glossary and scope.
Preparing for a pir.
Monitoring data.
Stakeholder engagement.
Evaluation evidence.
Case studies.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Practical Implications

Plan review evidence while the regulation is being designed, not only when the PIR deadline arrives.
Use monitoring and stakeholder evidence proportionately when causal comparison is infeasible.
State clearly what can and cannot be attributed to regulation using the available evidence.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Core Argument
The TIGER annex operationalises open science for government evaluation through proportionate planning, analysis, and reporting transparency.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Study Summary
The transparency annex adapts open science practices to government evaluation. It aims to make evaluation plans, analysis, and reporting transparent enough to strengthen integrity, trust, reuse, and value for money.
The annex is explicitly proportionate. Higher stakes, higher cost, high-profile, high learning value, novel, or complex evaluations should receive stronger transparency practices, while legal and data constraints still matter.
Planning transparency includes registration, evaluation plans, protocols, statistical analysis plans, timestamping, and archiving. These practices create a record before data collection or analysis can be shaped by results.
Analysis and reporting transparency require preserving materials, data, code, and methodological choices; communicating uncertainty; and showing whether findings are robust to reasonable alternative choices.
For close reading, track how this note uses transparency, open science, evaluation registry, and pre-registration while moving through transparency rationale, proportionality, and planning transparency. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Key Concepts

transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.
open science: Practices that make research plans, materials, analysis, and outputs more transparent and accessible.
evaluation registry: A transparency mechanism for recording planned, live, and completed government evaluations.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
analysis transparency: Preserving and, where proportionate, sharing the materials, data, code, and choices behind evaluation analysis.
reporting transparency: Clear reporting of methods, uncertainty, robustness, limitations, and interpretation.
robustness: The extent to which findings remain credible under reasonable checks, assumptions, and alternative analyses.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Appendix Structure

Transparency rationale.
Proportionality.
Planning transparency.
Analysis transparency.
Reporting transparency.
Tools and recommendations.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Practical Implications

Decide transparency practices during design, before data collection or analysis begins.
Archive plans, materials, code, and analysis decisions even when public release is not possible.
Report uncertainty and robustness in language decision-makers can understand.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Core Argument
Scoping turns an intervention, its theory of change, users, and uncertainties into answerable evaluation questions and a feasible design route.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 2 treats scoping as the disciplined bridge between policy need and evaluation design. Evaluators need to understand the intervention, its context, its theory of change, and what decisions the evaluation must inform.
A theory of change is used to identify assumptions, mechanisms, outcomes, data needs, and evidence gaps. The chapter stresses that evaluation approaches can be complementary: process evidence can support theory-based impact claims, and value for money work usually depends on impact evidence.
Evaluation questions are a control point. A long list of possible questions has to be prioritised into a smaller set of high-level questions that can guide design and remain manageable across delivery.
Scoping also includes practical feasibility: data access, timing, resources, stakeholder needs, ethical issues, transparency, and whether planned methods can answer the priority questions.
For close reading, track how this note uses scoping, theory of change, evaluation question, and evaluation design while moving through understanding the intervention, theory of change, and evaluation types and approaches. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Key Concepts

scoping: Early work to clarify the intervention, theory of change, users, questions, data, methods, and feasibility.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
evaluation question: A focused question that determines what evidence and analysis an evaluation must provide.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.
data access: The legal, ethical, technical, and practical ability to obtain data needed for evaluation questions.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Chapter Structure

Understanding the intervention.
Theory of change.
Evaluation types and approaches.
Evaluation questions.
Design and feasibility.
Registration.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Practical Implications

Use the theory of change to decide what evidence matters, not only to describe the programme.
Prioritise evaluation questions before committing to methods or supplier specifications.
Document design changes transparently when feasibility or evidence changes during implementation.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation data must be planned with the intervention, because evidence quality, access, linkage, and governance determine what questions can be answered.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 4 treats data planning as central to evaluation design. Data access, collection, and linkage need attention while the intervention is still being designed; otherwise the eventual evaluation may be weak, expensive, or impossible.
The chapter distinguishes existing administrative and monitoring data, long-term surveys, and new primary data collection. Existing data can reduce cost and burden, but it may not align exactly with evaluation questions or collect measures at the right time.
Triangulation strengthens confidence by combining sources such as monitoring data, surveys, interviews, social media data, and bespoke research. Data quality checks, piloting, bias reduction, and clear definitions matter across all sources.
The chapter also foregrounds data protection, access protocols, and data linking. Linking can enrich evaluation datasets, but it raises governance, security, legal, ethical, and practical issues that must be resolved early.
For close reading, track how this note uses data quality, data access, administrative data, and monitoring while moving through data requirements, existing and new data sources, and administrative and monitoring data. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Key Concepts

data quality: The fitness of evaluation data, including relevance, validity, completeness, timeliness, and bias control.
data access: The legal, ethical, technical, and practical ability to obtain data needed for evaluation questions.
administrative data: Operational data collected by public services or delivery systems that can support evaluation if fit for purpose.
monitoring: Routine tracking of delivery, outputs, performance, or benefits that can inform but does not replace evaluation.
triangulation: Using multiple evidence sources or methods to test, compare, and strengthen interpretation.
data linking: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Chapter Structure

Data requirements.
Existing and new data sources.
Administrative and monitoring data.
Data quality.
Data protection.
Linkage.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Practical Implications

Specify data needs during intervention design, not after delivery is complete.
Check whether existing administrative data measure the evaluation concepts directly enough.
Plan data sharing, linkage, ethics, and quality assurance before fieldwork or analysis starts.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation findings have value only if their users, decision points, products, publication route, and transparency commitments are planned from the start.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 6 treats use and dissemination as design issues, not communication tasks left until the report is drafted. Evaluators need to know which groups need what information, when they need it, and what form will make it usable.
The chapter distinguishes direct and indirect users. Direct users may need findings for live policy decisions, spending reviews, or delivery improvement; indirect users may need learning for future policy, scrutiny, or wider public understanding.
The dissemination plan should be agreed early with stakeholders, communication teams, and ministerial offices where relevant. This reduces surprises and protects publication decisions from appearing to depend on whether findings are favourable.
Openness and transparency are central. Registration, published protocols, clear reporting, accessible limitations, and publication expectations strengthen trust and public value.
For close reading, track how this note uses evaluation use, dissemination, transparency, and evaluation registry while moving through users and evidence needs, use and dissemination plan, and tailored reporting. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Key Concepts

evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.
dissemination: Planned communication of findings to audiences who need evidence for decisions, scrutiny, or learning.
transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.
evaluation registry: A transparency mechanism for recording planned, live, and completed government evaluations.
reporting transparency: Clear reporting of methods, uncertainty, robustness, limitations, and interpretation.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Chapter Structure

Users and evidence needs.
Use and dissemination plan.
Tailored reporting.
Publication.
Openness and transparency.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Practical Implications

Start dissemination planning when the evaluation is scoped, not when findings arrive.
Tailor outputs to audience decisions while keeping limitations and uncertainty visible.
Agree publication and transparency expectations early enough to protect credibility.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Core Argument
The executive summary positions evaluation as a government learning and accountability discipline that must combine evaluation types, methods, stages, and standards.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Study Summary
The summary frames evaluation as a way to understand whether interventions are designed well, delivered as intended, producing results, and offering value for money. It resists treating process, impact, and economic evaluation as isolated exercises.
Learning and accountability are the main reasons for evaluation. Learning helps government manage uncertainty, improve live interventions, and build evidence for future policy. Accountability helps departments explain outcomes, spending, and public value to decision-makers, scrutiny bodies, and the public.
The summary also acts as a route map. It identifies the core chapters on scoping, methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability, then points to annexes that deepen specialist topics such as analytical methods, quality, realist evaluation, complexity, transparency, AI, and test-and-learn practice.
For close reading, track how this note uses learning, accountability, process evaluation, and impact evaluation while moving through learning and accountability purposes, key evaluation terminology, and core chapter route. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, the executive summary is the orientation point: it explains why process, impact, and value for money evidence need to be planned together across the guidance.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Key Concepts

learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Executive Structure

Learning and accountability purposes.
Key evaluation terminology.
Core chapter route.
Annex and supplementary guidance route.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Practical Implications

Treat evaluation type as a design decision linked to the decision problem, not a label added after commissioning.
Plan process, impact, and value for money evidence together when the intervention needs an integrated judgment.
Use the chapter and annex route to find the right level of technical detail for the evaluation decision at hand.

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the expanded notes from Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2020).

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the expanded notes from Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2020).

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Plan evaluation with the intervention, business case, monitoring, and benefits management arrangements.
Start scoping with users, decisions, theory of change, assumptions, and priority evaluation questions.
Choose methods by question, causal claim, data feasibility, ethics, burden, and use rather than by familiarity.
Use mixed methods when process, impact, value for money, explanation, and implementation learning all matter.
Resolve data access, linkage, data quality, and protection issues before the evaluation depends on them.
Create governance that can quality assure design, tools, analysis, interpretation, and reporting while still adapting to change.
Plan dissemination, publication, and transparency at the start so findings can be used and trusted.
Use appendix guidance when specialist risks arise: complexity, realist explanation, AI interventions, qualitative quality, transparency, PIRs, or test-and-learn work.
Review capability before commissioning and build teams around the skills the evaluation actually requires.

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as checks during scoping, proposal decisions, design review, method selection, data planning, governance, reporting, publication, and capability development. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s context, stakes, resources, data constraints, stakeholder needs, and transparency obligations.

title: Core Concepts in Practical Program Evaluation
text:
Central Logic
Evaluation design should match a program’s stage, stakeholder needs, and theory of how action is expected to produce change.

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on selected notes from Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015).

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Begin by clarifying the program stage and intended evaluation purpose.
Use logic models for tractable program description and program theory when mechanisms and context matter.
Treat stakeholder assumptions as evaluation design evidence, not background noise.
Check whether a familiar logic-model format is hiding system-change or contextual complexity.

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Core Argument
The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Study Summary
The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.
Logic models help simplify a complicated program into components, while the schema gives evaluators a fuller way to address contextual factors and causal mechanisms.
The distinction matters because many evaluations fail when the representation is too simple for the program’s actual operating logic.

Use logic models for communication and initial structure.
Use the action model/change model schema when implementation arrangements and causal assumptions require more detail.
Choose the representation that supports evaluation decisions rather than the one stakeholders already know best.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 30-41 Chapter 1

This note summarises Chapter 1, “The Essence of Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 30-41 Chapter 1

This note summarises Chapter 1, “The Essence of Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The opening chapter uses a deliberately simple health example to show why an evaluation without program theory can identify an outcome pattern but cannot say much about why it occurred. A black-box evaluation can report whether results changed, but it gives weak guidance about which mechanisms worked, which activities mattered, or what should be changed next.
The chapter then reframes evaluation around a model of how resources, activities, outputs, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, assumptions, and contextual factors are connected. This makes the evaluation more diagnostic: results can be interpreted against the expected chain of contribution rather than treated as a single program-versus-outcome comparison.
A central practical point is that failure, partial success, and apparent success all need explanation. Program theory helps evaluators identify whether the intervention was implemented as intended, whether the causal logic was plausible, whether context disrupted the pathway, and whether observed results can be credibly attributed to the program.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Black-box evaluation: an evaluation that relates an intervention to outcomes without unpacking the intervening causal pathway.
Program theory: an explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to intended outcomes.
Implementation failure versus theory failure: a poor result may reflect weak delivery, weak causal assumptions, or both.
Partial success: evidence may support some links in the chain while exposing weak or missing links elsewhere.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Evaluation Without Program Theory
Evaluation With Program Theory
Learning from Failure
Learning from Partial Success
Learning from Success
Learning from “An Apple a Day”
Summary
Exercises

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Start evaluation design by asking what must be true for the intervention to work.
Collect evidence along the causal chain, not only at the final outcome.
Use the theory to diagnose why results occurred and what should be improved.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3

This note summarises Chapter 3, “Common Myths and Traps,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Core Argument
The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Study Summary
The chapter clears away myths that weaken practice: program theory is not brand new, there is not one correct way to do it, it can be a serious evaluation model, and it does not necessarily require excessive time. The authors also challenge the idea that a theory is untestable or that causality can simply be assumed once a model is drawn.
The traps are more operational. Program theory can become a compliance artifact, a vague outcomes list, an overcomplicated wall chart, or a model that ignores unintended outcomes and contextual variation. A particularly important trap is developing a theory for an evaluation and then failing to use it in evaluation design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.
The chapter’s stance is pragmatic: program theory should be purposeful, proportionate, explicit, and used. A modest but well-targeted theory is better than an elaborate representation that nobody uses to make decisions.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Key Concepts

Myth: a mistaken belief about what program theory is or requires.
Trap: a recurring practice problem that undermines the usefulness of a program theory.
Purposeful proportionality: investing enough effort for the intended use without turning the exercise into an end in itself.
Unintended results: outcomes that are outside the intended chain but still matter for evaluation.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:

Myth 7: Assume Causality
Traps To Avoid When Developing And Using Program Theory
Strategies to Avoid This Trap
Trap 2: Having a Poor Theory of Change
Trap 3: Poorly Specifying Intended Results
Trap 4: Ignoring Unintended Results
Trap 5: Oversimplifying

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Practical Implications

Choose the level of detail to match the decision or evaluation use.
Build in explicit attention to unintended outcomes and alternative explanations.
Make sure the final theory controls actual choices about measures, methods, and reporting.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 96-121 Chapter 5

This note summarises Chapter 5, “The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Core Argument
The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Study Summary
The chapter introduces distinctions among simple, complicated, and complex interventions. Simple situations have relatively direct and stable pathways. Complicated situations involve multiple components, actors, sites, or technical dependencies. Complex situations involve adaptation, emergence, feedback, and context-sensitive pathways.
The authors use these distinctions to warn against forcing every intervention into a linear model. A program theory may need to show multiple strands, variable implementation, different causal mechanisms in different settings, or pathways that emerge through learning. Governance and consistency also matter because centrally controlled programs differ from networked or locally adapted interventions.
The chapter’s practical value is diagnostic. Before choosing a representation or evaluation design, evaluators should ask what kind of intervention they are dealing with and what type of causal claim is reasonable.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Key Concepts

Simple, complicated, complex: a continuum for thinking about intervention structure and causal predictability.
Necessariness and sufficiency: whether a component is required for success and whether it can generate success on its own.
Change trajectory: the expected pattern and timing of change.
Governance and consistency: how control, variation, and adaptation affect the theory.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:

Summary
Exercise

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Practical Implications

Do not use a single linear chain when the intervention has interacting strands or adaptive pathways.
Use context and governance analysis to decide how general the theory can be.
Treat complexity as a design issue for monitoring and evaluation, not an excuse for vagueness.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 176-225 Chapter 7

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 176-225 Chapter 7

This note summarises Chapter 7, “Developing a Theory of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Core Argument
A theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem analysis to intended outcomes; it requires situation analysis, evidence-informed causal reasoning, careful focusing, and a defensible outcomes chain.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Study Summary
The chapter focuses on the change side of program theory: what problem is being addressed, what causes or contributing factors sustain it, what opportunities can be used, and what sequence of outcomes would indicate meaningful change. Situation analysis is presented as the foundation for a credible theory of change.
The authors stress that causes, consequences, and opportunities should be investigated rather than assumed. Research evidence can inform which causal factors are plausible and which strategies are likely to influence them. Focusing and scoping are then needed because no program can address every cause, consequence, population, and context at once.
The outcomes chain is the central device in the chapter. It connects early, intermediate, and long-term outcomes in a way that shows consequential progression rather than just listing desired results. A good outcomes chain makes the causal story inspectable and testable.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Situation analysis: structured understanding of the problem, causes, consequences, and opportunities.
Outcomes chain: a sequence of linked outcomes showing expected progression toward longer-term change.
Focusing and scoping: deliberate choices about what the program will and will not address.
Causal plausibility: the extent to which the proposed pathway is supported by reasoning and evidence.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:

Features Of A Theory Of Change

Questions for Preparing a Situation Analysis
The Nature and Extent of the Main Problem That the Program Addresses
Causes and Contributing Factors
Consequences
How the Use of These Questions Might Vary with Choice of Program Theory Development Process
Using Research To Identify Causes And Select Strategies
Assessing Complication and Complexity
Focusing And Scoping

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Do not start with activities; start with the problem and causal conditions.
Use research and local evidence to identify leverage points.
Build outcomes chains that distinguish immediate outputs from meaningful changes in people, systems, or conditions.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 268-319 Chapter 9

This note summarises Chapter 9, “Representing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Representing program theory is a design choice: diagrams, matrices, narratives, and multiple models should be selected to fit the intervention’s causal structure, complexity, and intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This chapter surveys representation options, including pipeline logic models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and multiple linked models. It treats representation as more than presentation: the form chosen shapes what users notice, question, and can use.
The authors distinguish representations for complicated and complex programs. Multiple organizations, sequential interventions, parallel causal strands, context-specific mechanisms, emergent theory of action, vertical integration, and systems concepts may all require alternatives to a single diagram.
The chapter ends with practical quality criteria. Good representations explain contribution, avoid dead ends, make arrows meaningful, show direction of change, focus on key elements, avoid clutter, and remove material that does not add meaning. The test is whether the representation clarifies the theory for its intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline logic model: a representation moving from inputs and activities through outputs and outcomes.
Outcomes chain model: a representation centered on consequential outcome progression.
Realist matrix: a representation that links context, mechanism, and outcome patterns.
Representational economy: including enough to guide use without overwhelming interpretation.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Options For Representation
A Pipeline Logic Model of a Computer Project
An Outcomes Chain Logic Model of a Computer Project
Comparing Pipeline Models and Outcomes Chain Models
Realist Matrices
Narratives
Developing More Than One Logic Model
Options for Formatting Logic Models
Representing Complicated Program Theory
Multiple Organizations Working in Parallel
Multiple Interventions Working Together Sequentially
Multiple Causal Strands Within an Intervention
Interventions That Work Only in Particular Contexts
Representing Complex Program Theory

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Choose representation after clarifying use and intervention complexity.
Use multiple models when one view cannot serve all users or levels.
Review diagrams for meaningful arrows, missing assumptions, and avoidable clutter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 346-377 Chapter 11

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 346-377 Chapter 11

This note summarises Chapter 11, “Some Research-Based Theories of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Core Argument
Research-based theories of change can strengthen program theory when they are selected critically and adapted to the intervention context rather than imported mechanically.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Study Summary
This chapter introduces several families of research-based theory, including reasoned action and planned behavior, stages of change, empowerment, diffusion, socioecological theory, and network theory. Each offers concepts that can help explain how change might occur.
The authors do not present these theories as universal templates. Each has limits, assumptions, and domains of usefulness. The task is to use research-based theory to sharpen causal reasoning, identify intermediate outcomes, and choose measures, while still checking whether the theory fits the specific program and context.
The chapter is especially useful as a bridge between general social science and practical evaluation. It shows how evaluators can use established theory to avoid purely local speculation without losing attention to implementation realities.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Research-based theory: formal or empirical theory that can inform causal assumptions in a program theory.
Diffusion: the spread of ideas or practices through populations, networks, or systems.
Socioecological theory: attention to multiple levels of influence, from individual to institutional and environmental.
Theory selection: choosing concepts because they explain the intervention, not because they are familiar.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Theory Of Reasoned Action And Theory Of Planned Behavior

Stages Of Change Theory
Some Limitations
Empowerment Theory
Diffusion Theory
Key Concepts and Related Theories
Relevance for Building a Program Theory and Measuring Outcomes
Socioecological Theory
Network Theory
Selecting And Using Theories Of Change
Summary
Exercises

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Use formal theory to identify mechanisms, assumptions, and intermediate outcomes.
State the limitations of imported theory and adapt it to context.
Avoid mixing theories without explaining how their mechanisms relate.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 414-443 Chapter 13

This note summarises Chapter 13, “Logic Models Resources,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Core Argument
Logic model resources and technologies can support representation, but the usefulness of a logic model depends more on conceptual clarity than on the drawing tool.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Study Summary
This chapter reviews pipeline logic models, variations of outcomes chain models, drawn models, standard software, specialist visual tools, and dedicated logic model packages. It gives evaluators a practical sense of available representation formats.
The chapter reinforces earlier cautions: a visually tidy model can still be conceptually weak, and a complex tool can obscure rather than clarify the theory. Technology should support the intended use, audience, and complexity of the program theory.
The practical emphasis is on choosing the right representation environment. Some situations need quick sketches to support dialogue; others need formal models for documentation, monitoring, or reporting. The tool should make revision, communication, and use easier.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline model variation: different ways to organize inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes.
Outcomes-chain variation: different degrees of detail, nesting, and temporal structure in outcome pathways.
Representation technology: software or visual methods used to create and maintain logic models.
Tool fit: alignment between representation medium, audience, and purpose.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 496-527 Chapter 15

This note summarises Chapter 15, “Causal Inference,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Core Argument
Causal inference in program theory should be both scientific and pragmatic, combining congruence analysis, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review to assess whether the program plausibly contributed to observed outcomes.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Study Summary
The chapter argues against polarized thinking about causal inference. Strong causal analysis requires rigor, but rigor does not always mean one design. Program theory helps evaluators choose and combine methods that are appropriate to the intervention, question, and available evidence.
The authors organize causal analysis around congruence, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review. Congruence examines whether observed patterns match the theory across intermediate and final outcomes. Counterfactual comparisons ask what would have happened without the program. Critical review tests alternative explanations, exceptions, and weak links.
The chapter is especially important for complicated and complex interventions, where single average effects may hide variation. Program theory supports disaggregation, mechanism testing, and careful explanation of why outcomes occurred for whom and under what conditions.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Key Concepts

Congruence analysis: checking whether evidence across the causal chain fits the program theory.
Counterfactual comparison: estimating what would have happened in the absence of the intervention.
Critical review: actively examining alternative explanations and exceptions.
Scientific pragmatism: using credible methods that fit the real evaluation problem.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:

Counterfactual Comparisons

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Practical Implications

Do not treat program theory as a substitute for causal evidence; use it to structure causal tests.
Combine pattern evidence, comparison evidence, and alternative-explanation review.
Disaggregate results where the theory predicts different pathways or effects.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Practical Habits

Start by naming the intended users, uses, and timing of the program theory.
Separate the problem analysis and desired change pathway from the program’s activities and delivery model.
Make assumptions explicit enough that they can be tested or monitored.
Choose representation formats for use, not for visual polish.
Critique the theory before using it to select indicators or evaluation methods.
Use the theory to distinguish implementation failure, theory failure, contextual disruption, and partial success.
Report evidence along the causal pathway so conclusions are traceable.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Design Checklist

What decision or learning need should the program theory support?
Who needs to participate in developing, challenging, or using it?
What problem, causes, contributing factors, and opportunities does the theory identify?
Which outcomes form a genuine causal chain rather than a list of aspirations?
Which activities and outputs are expected to influence the causal factors?
What contextual or external factors could enable or block outcomes?
Which links in the theory are most uncertain, consequential, or worth testing?
What comparison, congruence evidence, or critical review is needed for causal inference?

title: Strategic Qualitative Principles in Practice
text:
Core Argument
The section frames qualitative inquiry as a set of strategic principles that must be adapted to practical field conditions.

title: Strategic Qualitative Principles in Practice
text:
Study Summary
The section frames qualitative inquiry as a set of strategic principles that must be adapted to practical field conditions.
It rejects the idea that qualitative methods only belong in ideal situations; their value often appears when evaluation questions require context-sensitive judgment.
The evaluator’s task is to maintain qualitative discipline while adjusting to real constraints in access, time, and use.

Plan for adaptation while protecting documentation and analytic transparency.
Use qualitative design choices to make trade-offs explicit.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Core Argument
The section shows how qualitative inquiry can test and refine program models by connecting processes, implementation, and outcomes.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Study Summary
The section shows how qualitative inquiry can test and refine program models by connecting processes, implementation, and outcomes.
Qualitative evidence is useful where the formal theory of change is incomplete, contested, or too abstract to explain practice.
This makes qualitative methods a complement to logic models: they can reveal how the model is experienced and enacted.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Practical Implications

Use interviews and observations to test whether the stated program model matches implementation.
Look for mechanisms and unanticipated outcomes that the initial theory missed.
Treat qualitative findings as evidence for revising the theory of change.

title: Core Concepts in Realistic Evaluation
text:
Central Logic
Realist evaluation studies programs through context-mechanism-outcome configurations rather than asking only whether a whole program worked.

title: Core Concepts in Realistic Evaluation

text: This practice synthesis draws on selected notes from Realistic Evaluation (Pawson and Tilley 1997).

title: Realistic Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Specify conjectured CMO configurations before selecting data collection methods.
Analyze variation across contexts and participants instead of averaging it away too quickly.
Use evaluation findings to refine program theory for future adaptation.
Report findings as conditional explanations, not universal effects.

title: Realistic Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:
Core Argument
This section turns realist philosophy into an evaluation task: identify, articulate, test, and refine CMO configurations.

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:
Study Summary
This section turns realist philosophy into an evaluation task: identify, articulate, test, and refine CMO configurations.
The emphasis is on hypotheses about how mechanisms operate under social and cultural conditions.
Realist evidence therefore depends on comparing where the same mechanism appears to work differently.
